briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon, everyone. I will now begin the climate briefing for June 24, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Global Heatwaves and Worsening Climate Crisis
sections.content: Today, record-breaking heatwaves and their severe impacts have been reported across various parts of the world. As intense heatwaves swept across Europe, the German economy incurred billions of euros in losses [38, 39, 47, 52, 59]. The UK recorded its highest-ever June temperature, and some parts of France experienced temperatures nearing 40°C [74, 75]. Consequently, emergency situations are arising across society, including the cancellation of a climate change event on heatwave adaptation in London [40, 48, 76]. According to new analyses, the number of dangerously humid heat days globally has more than doubled [49, 63]. Scientists have warned that the media is overlooking the 'fundamental' link between heatwaves and climate change, emphasizing the direct impacts of the climate crisis [65].

sections.title: 🌍 International Climate Action and Policy Trends
sections.content: The international community is facing various challenges while simultaneously pursuing policy efforts to address climate change. Ambassador Kyun Jong-ho for Climate Change attended the 10th Ministerial Meeting on Climate Action (MoCA10), emphasizing the importance of implementing the Paris Agreement and underscoring the necessity of international cooperation [3, 4]. In the UK, there have been policy advancements, including Parliament passing the 7th Carbon Budget into law and the Climate Change Committee (CCC) publishing an updated report on net-zero progress [64, 73]. However, the Environmental Audit Committee responded by stating that greater ambition for electrification is needed [69]. Meanwhile, the 2026 Bonn Climate Change Conference reportedly found it difficult to make progress [51], and ShareAction challenged GFANZ on climate adaptation finance issues, revealing challenges in the international financial sector [71].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Climate Change Response and Carbon Neutrality Efforts
sections.content: Domestically, diverse efforts are actively underway to address climate change and achieve carbon neutrality. The Greenhouse Gas Inventory & Research Center and the Korean Society of Climate Change Research are discussing responses to the transition of the international climate statistics system [1], while a lively debate on AI and climate change took place at a youth science exploration competition [2]. At the local community level, 'Borabori' is being cultivated as a specialized crop for climate change response in Yeosu, Jeollanam-do [5], and regional cooperation is being strengthened with the official launch of the 'Jeonnam-Gwangju Climate Crisis Response Council (JGCC),' led by the Gwangju Regional Meteorological Administration and involving 51 organizations [7]. LH unveiled carbon-neutral future housing technologies and a blueprint for future cities at the Land, Infrastructure and Transport Technology Fair [14, 18, 20, 21, 23, 27]. Pohang City established an industry-academia-research-government cooperation system to foster a 43.1 billion KRW 'carbon-neutral climate tech industry' [16, 26, 28].

sections.title: 🌱 Energy Transition and Renewable Energy Expansion
sections.content: International cooperation strategies to simultaneously achieve energy security and carbon neutrality were actively discussed at the Jeju Forum, emphasizing that these two objectives must be pursued complementarily [11, 13, 22, 25, 36, 37]. Technological innovation and the spread of renewable energy are also noteworthy. China has completed a mega-project combining offshore solar power, hydrogen production, and energy storage [32], and solar fences are emerging as a new solution for energy independence [33]. Domestically, Yeongam County is enhancing understanding of renewable energy through its 'Visiting Sunlight Classroom' program [34], and the designer of Haenam Solaseado proposed that renewable energy in the Honam region could be a solution to semiconductor power issues [29]. However, a warning has also been raised regarding the need to prepare for a 'renewable energy drought' in the power mix during the climate crisis era [31], underscoring the importance of meticulous strategy formulation for a stable energy transition.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing acknowledged the severity of global heatwaves and highlighted the cooperation of the international community, along with the active efforts of various domestic and international stakeholders towards carbon neutrality and renewable energy transition. This is a time that calls for sustained attention and action to address the climate crisis. This concludes the climate briefing for June 24, 2026.

articles.title: UK records its hottest June day and France its hottest day ever as heatwave sweeps Europe
articles.description: Temperature of 36.1C (97F) recorded in Hampshire, while two-thirds of Europe’s population experience temperatures above 30CThe UK has broken its all-time temperature record for June and France has recorded its hottest day ever for the second day running, as a heatwave affecting more than 90 million people sweeps across swathes of Europe.As the UK and France registered record-breaking temperatures, the World Health Organization warned that the extreme temperatures are “putting lives at risk”. Con

summary.phenomenon: A heatwave has swept across Europe, with the UK recording its highest June temperature ever and France experiencing its hottest day ever for the second consecutive day. In Hampshire, UK, 36.1°C (97°F) was recorded, while over two-thirds of Europe's population experienced temperatures above 30°C, affecting more than 90 million people.
summary.cause: Not mentioned in the article. (Only referred to as a phenomenon caused by a 'heatwave').
summary.outlook: The World Health Organization (WHO) has warned that these extreme temperatures are 'putting lives at risk'.

articles.original_title: Europe heatwave live: UK breaks temperature record for June as parts of France hit 40C – as it happened
articles.description: West Sussex reports temperature of 35.8C, beating previous record from 1976; red weather alert extended to 72 of France’s 96 mainland departmentsGrahame Madge, a Met Office spokesperson, said the agency is forecasting 39C as a headline maximum temperature on Thursday in the UK, most likely for somewhere in London or the south-east.“It is possible we could see temperatures higher than the 39C if the final values are at the upper end of our narrow range,” he said, according to the Press Associatio

summary.phenomenon: A temperature of 35.8°C was reported in West Sussex, UK, breaking the previous record from 1976, and a red weather alert has been extended to 72 of France's 96 mainland departments.
summary.outlook: A UK Met Office spokesperson forecasted a maximum temperature of 39°C for Thursday in the UK, most likely in London or the South-East, adding that temperatures higher than 39°C are possible if the final values reach the upper end of their narrow forecast range.

articles.original_title: ‘It’s dangerous’: how schools, care homes and other UK workplaces are coping in searing heat
articles.description: From bus drivers struggling to focus to those hauling scaffolding under a blazing sun, workers say risks must be taken seriouslyAs temperatures in the UK hit record levels for June, people are being advised to avoid exercise and unnecessary travel. So how do you work in this heat?We look at how various sectors of the economy are coping with unprecedented temperatures, and how working practices will have to adapt to increasingly frequent heatwaves that are predicted to be longer and more intense

summary.phenomenon: As June temperatures in the UK reached record levels, various professions, including bus drivers struggling to focus and workers hauling scaffolding under the blazing sun, are experiencing difficulties due to the heatwave.
summary.cause: Not mentioned in the article. (Only referred to as a phenomenon caused by 'record-level June temperatures').
summary.outlook: People are advised to avoid exercise and unnecessary travel. Various sectors of the economy will need to adapt their coping mechanisms and working practices to increasingly frequent, longer, and more intense heatwaves, which are predicted to occur due to unprecedented temperatures.
